front: Painting: over 100 paintings of alternating black ovals and rectangular bars on white, inspired by the Spanish Civil War
back: Elegy to the Spanish Republic (Robert Motherwell)

front: Painting: first entry titled after a Lorca poem 'At Five in the Afternoon'; original title changed because New Yorkers associated it with cocktails
back: Elegy to the Spanish Republic (Robert Motherwell)

front: Painting: called 'a funeral for something one cared about' and 'a private insistence that a terrible death happened that should not be forgot'
back: Elegy to the Spanish Republic (Robert Motherwell)

front: Painting: grew out of a 1948 drawing for a Harold Rosenberg poem in Possibilities magazine; inspired by André Malraux's speech at a San Francisco rally
back: Elegy to the Spanish Republic (Robert Motherwell)

front: Painting: shapes described by artist as resembling a dead bull's testicles; most famous entries are the 34th, 108th, and 110th
back: Elegy to the Spanish Republic (Robert Motherwell)

front: Artist: edited the Documents of Modern Art series
Artist: helped inspire Fluxus with an anthology called Dada Poets and Painters
Artist: created the nearly monochromatic Open series and the Je t'aime series with cursive French text
Artist: co-edited the art journal Possibilities with Harold Rosenberg
Artist: encouraged by Marxist critic Meyer Schapiro, who wrote 'From Mozarabic to Romanesque in Silos'
Artist: co-founded the Fine Arts Work Center in Provincetown, Massachusetts with poet Stanley Kunitz
Artist: Roberto Matta taught this artist automatism in 1941 after a boat trip to Mexico
Painting: consists of thick black brushstrokes and splatters surrounded by faint orange halos; title references a paint solvent
